Standalone Concept Overlap Analysis

Generated: 2026-03-04 21:31 | 22 standalone dimensions | Layers 6-40 | 1000 bootstrap iterations

Summary: This measures how much standalone concept activation patterns overlap in the model's representation space. Unlike the contrast overlap analysis (2f), there is no human-vs-AI framing — each concept is presented independently. All results use centered vectors (global mean subtracted) to remove the shared component that dominates raw mean vectors. Dims 16 (human) and 17 (AI) serve as entity references.

Methods

Pipeline Overview

This analysis measures how much the standalone concept mean vectors of different dimensions overlap in LLaMA-2-13B's representation space. Unlike the contrast analysis (2f), there is no human-vs-AI framing here. Each concept is presented independently (e.g., "Think about phenomenological experience"), and we measure cosine similarity between the mean activation vectors of different concepts.

Key Difference from Contrast Overlap (2f)

Contrast (2f)Standalone (2g)
InputHuman- and AI-framed promptsUnframed concept prompts
Vectormean(human) - mean(AI) per layermean(all prompts) per layer
Overlap means"Do these dims define human-vs-AI the same way?""Do these concepts activate similar patterns?"
N dims19 (with dim 0 baseline)22 (no baseline; has entity + sysprompt variants)
Entity referenceDim 0 (explicit baseline)Dim 16 (human) and dim 17 (AI)

Data

  • 22 standalone dimensions, each with ~40 concept prompts (no human/AI labels)
  • 41 layers, each 5120-dimensional
  • Layers 6-40 used for summary statistics

Computation

  1. Mean vector: For each dimension d and layer L, compute mean_d[L] = mean(all prompt activations at layer L).
  2. Centering: Compute global_mean[L] = mean across all dimensions of mean_d[L]. Then centered_d[L] = mean_d[L] - global_mean[L]. This removes the shared "abstract concept processing" component that dominates raw mean vectors.
  3. Pairwise overlap: For each pair (i, j) and each layer L, compute |cos(centered_i[L], centered_j[L])|, average across layers 6-40.
  4. Bootstrap CIs: 1000 iterations. Each iteration resamples prompts with replacement for each dimension, recomputes mean vectors, subtracts that iteration's global mean, then recomputes pairwise |cos|.

Why Centering?

Raw mean vectors for standalone concepts are dominated by a large shared component — all concept prompts produce similar average activations because they share similar prompt structure and abstract reasoning. This makes all raw pairwise cosines ~0.96, masking real differences between concepts. Centering (subtracting the global mean across all dimensions) removes this shared component and reveals concept-specific structure: centered overlaps have mean ~0.49, std ~0.22, range 0.05-0.92 — comparable to contrast results.

This is analogous to mean-centering in PCA or removing the first principal component. The raw results are preserved for reference.

Chance Level

E[|cos|] for random 5120-d vectors = 0.0112.

1. Pairwise Overlap Matrix (Centered)

Mean |cosine similarity| between centered standalone concept mean vectors, averaged across layers 6-40. Centering removes the shared component across all concepts. Dimensions grouped by category.

What This Shows

Each cell shows the mean |cosine similarity| between two standalone concepts' centered mean activation vectors, averaged across layers 6-40. Centering removes the shared component that makes all raw cosines ~0.96, revealing concept-specific structure.

Key Findings

Highest-overlap pairs:

  1. SysPrompt (talkto human) x SysPrompt (talkto AI): 0.923
  2. Embodiment x Animacy: 0.910
  3. Phenomenology x Emotions: 0.901
  4. SysPrompt (bare human) x SysPrompt (bare AI): 0.894
  5. Expertise x Helpfulness: 0.893

Lowest-overlap pairs:

  1. Roles x Shapes: 0.075
  2. Shapes x AI: 0.071
  3. Cognitive x AI: 0.067
  4. Roles x Biological: 0.065
  5. Helpfulness x AI: 0.055

Mean off-diagonal overlap: 0.485 (std: 0.224, chance: 0.011)

Raw vs Centered Comparison

Why Centering Matters

The raw (uncentered) heatmap below shows all pairwise overlaps ~0.96. This is because standalone concept mean vectors are dominated by a large shared component — all concept prompts produce similar average activations due to shared prompt structure and abstract reasoning patterns. This shared component acts as a "DC offset" that overwhelms concept-specific variation.

Centering (subtracting the global mean across all dimensions at each layer) removes this shared component, analogous to removing the first principal component or mean-centering before PCA. The centered analysis above reveals rich variation: some concept pairs are highly aligned (e.g., related mental capacities) while others are nearly orthogonal (e.g., shapes vs entity concepts).

Note: This issue does not affect the contrast analysis (2f) because contrast vectors (human_mean - AI_mean) already subtract out the shared component within each dimension.

Raw (uncentered) overlap matrix for reference. All values ~0.96 due to the shared component dominating mean vectors.

2. Entity Reference Overlap

Overlap with Human (dim 16) and AI (dim 17) standalone concepts. Error bars: 95% bootstrap CI. Red dashed line: chance (0.0112).

What This Shows

How much each standalone concept's centered mean activation pattern resembles the standalone "human" concept (dim 16) and "AI" concept (dim 17). These serve as entity-level references analogous to the baseline (dim 0) in the contrast analysis, but derived from standalone concept prompts rather than entity-framing contrasts.

Interpretation

  • High human overlap, low AI overlap: Concept activates patterns more similar to "human" than "AI"
  • High overlap with both: Concept is broadly related to entity representations
  • Low overlap with both: Concept is orthogonal to entity identity (e.g., shapes, formality)

3. Human vs AI Reference Comparison

Each point is a standalone concept. X-axis: overlap with Human concept. Y-axis: overlap with AI concept. Diagonal = equal overlap.

What This Shows

Each point is a standalone concept, plotted by its overlap with the "human" concept (x-axis) vs "AI" concept (y-axis). Points on the diagonal have equal overlap with both entity concepts.

Interpretation

  • Above diagonal: More similar to AI than human
  • Below diagonal: More similar to human than AI
  • Far from origin: Strongly related to entity concepts
  • Near origin: Entity-orthogonal concept

4. Layer-Resolved Overlap Profiles

Cosine similarity (signed) between selected standalone concept pairs at each layer. Red dashed line marks layer 6.

What This Shows

Signed cosine similarity at each layer for selected standalone concept pairs. Positive = similar direction, negative = opposite direction.

Selected Pairs

  • Human vs Phenomenology: Does the "human" concept align with phenomenological experience?
  • AI vs Phenomenology: Same question for the AI concept
  • Human vs AI: How similar are the two entity concepts themselves?
  • Human vs Shapes: Negative control — should be low
  • Phenomenology vs Emotions: Two closely related mental concepts
  • Phenomenology vs Embodiment: Cross-category (Mental vs Physical)
  • Human vs Biological: Humans are biological — does the model reflect this?
  • SysPrompt talkto vs bare: Different prompt styles for same entity

Red dashed line: layer 6 cutoff.

5. Category-Level Summary

Mean pairwise |cosine| within each category (solid) vs between categories (hatched).

What This Shows

Average pairwise |cosine| within each category (solid) vs between categories (hatched). Higher within-category overlap suggests the model groups these concepts similarly.

Categories

  • Mental (dims 1-7, 18): Core mental capacities
  • Physical (dims 8-10): Embodiment, roles, animacy
  • Pragmatic (dims 11-13): Formality, expertise, helpfulness
  • Entity (dims 16-17): Human and AI standalone concepts
  • Bio Ctrl (dim 14): Biological processes
  • Shapes (dim 15): Geometric shapes
  • SysPrompt (dims 20-23): System prompt variants
